decomposition.title: RFP - Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise IT, hospitality, wellness, digital innovation
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: Not explicitly stated. Inferred potential opportunity approximately USD $15M-$75M over 3-5 years based on enterprise-scale hosting options for a large Wisconsin data center, SAP ecosystem, VMware estate, Windows/Linux servers, databases, storage, backup/DR, network connectivity, security tooling, and possible DCaaS/cloud/hybrid transition. Phase 0 advisory-only portion would be materially smaller, likely USD $250K-$1.5M if contracted separately.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. The document is for a Phase 0 evaluation exercise; inferred commercial planning horizon is likely 3-5 years for hosting/TCO comparison based on enterprise data-center exit, hosting, SAP/non-SAP workload pricing, managed services cost views, and efficiency-over-time requirements.

requirements.text: Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel; RFP responses/proposals are due to Kohler on 26 June, and all communication must be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Late or misdirected submission can make the proposal non-compliant before evaluation; the required submission channel is outside the proposal section and tied to contact details.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit responses using the Vendor Response template and complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A superficially completed template or Yes/No-only response is expressly stated to be insufficient, creating a response-format disqualification risk.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory certification-evidence requirement is located in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements section and the exact certification list may be in an external template not provided.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards; specific regions/standards not stated.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications not named in the provided document.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Conditional hard gate for DCaaS providers; the document points to the Vendor Response template for more information, which is not included.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable to DCaaS providers; industry standards not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers; exact attestations not named in the provided document.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency compliance is an easy-to-miss pricing instruction; non-USD pricing could make the commercial response non-comparable or non-compliant.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must cover landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must cover environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must cover identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must cover hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must cover region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is explicitly required as part of the solution response but no acceptable locations are stated; the missing detail may be in external templates or subsequent phases.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach required; no specific allowed/prohibited jurisdictions stated.

requirements.text: Vendors must cover disaster recovery, high availability and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, including Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.

In-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center; COTS and internally developed applications; Windows and Linux virtual machines; databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL and other supported platforms; middleware and integration platforms; file storage systems and shared infrastructure services; backup and disaster recovery solutions; identity and access management integrations; network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments; monitoring, logging and security tooling; and selected OT-adjacent workloads limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset and use it as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory baseline is external to the provided document; failure to use it correctly may invalidate sizing and pricing.

requirements.text: Vendors must highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information and clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data gaps must be surfaced rather than silently assumed, which can affect pricing defensibility and evaluation.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, constraints, deviations, and additional assumptions associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
Pricing must be transparent, structured and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of the RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs; application and infrastructure hosting costs; migration / implementation costs if applicable; and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: SAP/non-SAP cost separation is a specific commercial-format requirement that can be missed if pricing is rolled up at platform level.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Kohler requires efficiency gains and protections based on actual prices paid, not generic list-price discounts; this may conflict with standard vendor commercial models.

requirements.text: Payment terms are an important part of the proposal, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Payment terms below net 90 are explicitly stated to be not competitive; this is a major hidden commercial risk even if not worded as automatic rejection.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities, shared responsibility framework, governance, security, compliance approach, and operations and support model.
Proposed models should demonstrate support for a progressive transition away from the current Data Center footprint while maintaining business continuity and operational stability.

Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of the proposed program governance model, including indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework, and key roles and responsibilities.

All information shared as part of the RFP is confidential; vendors must use Kohler information solely for responding to the RFP and must not disclose, distribute, or share information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality terms are in the appendix and restrict sharing with third parties; vendors using subcontractors, hyperscalers, or offshore solution teams may need prior written consent before disclosure.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This broad compliance obligation appears in the Legal & Compliance appendix and may incorporate enterprise IT, manufacturing, regulatory, and data protection obligations not enumerated in the main RFP.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.

requirements.text: Responses are expected to support solution feasibility, approach, and business case development, not detailed implementation commitments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP is Phase 0 only and expressly excludes execution commitments; proposing full execution as if in scope may misalign the bid.

requirements.text: Vendors should treat the listed execution activities as out of scope for this RFP phase and factor them in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Detailed migration execution, implementation, cutover, refactoring, low-level design, steady-state managed services delivery commitments, execution of security controls/audits, tool implementation, training, data cleansing, and testing execution are out of scope for this Phase 0 response unless only approach/methodology or illustrative capability is requested.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout
similar_deals.why_relevant: Closest historical win: manufacturing, USA plus global footprint, SAP ecosystem, enterprise scale, and similar 3-5 year transformation profile. Relevant to Kohler's SAP hosting, migration, and operating-continuity concerns.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Executive sponsor alignment and credible SAP scale were decisive; manufacturing SAP references, zero-downtime methodology, and global delivery helped win.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Similar manufacturing IT transformation size and duration, with ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II requirements. Directly relevant because Polaris has mandatory submission, certification, DCaaS-attestation, and response-template requirements.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost because a local entity requirement was missed in the compliance appendix; mandatory compliance evidence must be treated as a bid gate.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Manufacturing enterprise IT transformation with similar commercial scale and duration. Reinforces that compliance mechanics can override technical strength.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Repeated loss pattern: missed local/entity compliance requirement. A red-team compliance review is mandatory before submission.

similar_deals.title: Supply Chain AI Platform
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to Kohler's digital innovation, enterprise integration, multi-region operations, SAP/Oracle ecosystem, and need to differentiate beyond generic hosting capability.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Proprietary accelerators, pre-built SAP/Oracle connectors, and measurable timeline reduction beat lower hourly rates.

similar_deals.title: Telecom Network Automation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Industry differs, but it is relevant to US enterprise infrastructure, network connectivity, managed operations, and the need for strong US references for a Wisconsin data-center/hybrid-hosting decision.
similar_deals.key_lesson: US enterprise buyers valued direct peer references and strong infrastructure/network credentials.

similar_deals.title: Healthcare IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Useful pricing-risk comparator for Polaris because the RFP emphasizes transparent, structured, itemized pricing and TCO comparison across hosting options.
similar_deals.key_lesson: A better technical solution lost because pricing was 20% above the winning bid; commercial sharpness matters as much as architecture.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 46% with medium-low confidence. The positive evidence is strong but not sufficient for a majority probability: past proposal intelligence contains an almost exact Kohler Project Polaris solution pattern, including a 12-week Hosting Decision Lab, vendor-neutral cloud/DCaaS/hybrid comparison, SAP/VMware/database/DR/network/security coverage, USD TCO, and downstream pricing patterns around $24.75M-$30.67M for 36 months. The deal corpus also contains a highly relevant manufacturing SAP win, DEAL-003, at $71M over 4 years, showing that SAP scale, manufacturing credibility, and global delivery can win comparable enterprise transformations. However, there is no public procurement data, so there is no ground-truth competitor pricing or award trend to validate assumptions. The strongest proposal intelligence is WIP content for this same RFP, not proof of a prior Kohler win. The deal corpus also contains two relevant manufacturing IT losses, DEAL-056 and DEAL-100, caused by missed compliance requirements, which directly maps to Polaris eliminators around template completion, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, and submission mechanics. DEAL-043 adds pricing downside because technically superior enterprise IT bids can lose when 20% overpriced. No evidence confirms a Kohler relationship, executive sponsor, incumbent advantage, or detailed evaluation weighting. Therefore the opportunity is winnable if compliance, references, architecture evidence, and commercial transparency are fixed, but the honest probability remains below 50% absent procurement validation or buyer-access evidence.
capability_gaps: Mandatory certification evidence is not present in the supplied intelligence. The RFP requires vendors to demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards with certification evidence; this must be supplied, not asserted.

If proposing DCaaS, explicit compliance attestations from the DCaaS provider are required. The current intelligence describes a compliance-attestation matrix but does not provide signed/provider-specific DCaaS attestations.

The detailed Vendor Response / Evaluation Template is not available in the supplied intelligence. Because the RFP states simple Yes/No answers are insufficient, failure to map narrative answers, evidence, diagrams, and assumptions into the required template is a pass/fail risk.

Current-state Kohler workload inventory, dependency maps, RTO/RPOs, run costs, contracts, and incumbent responsibilities are not available. This creates a gap against the requirement to provide credible cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid assumptions and defensible 3-5 year TCO.

Architecture evidence must be completed. Past proposal content references landing-zone blueprints and target-state diagrams, but the submitted response must include concrete multi-account/multi-subscription landing zone, environment segregation, IAM integration, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency, DR/HA, monitoring/logging, and security reference designs.

No evidence was provided of a direct Kohler executive sponsor relationship or incumbent advantage. DEAL-003 shows sponsor alignment mattered in a comparable win, so this is a competitive gap rather than a technical gap.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Polaris Hosting Decision Lab: position Phase 0 as a Kohler-owned, evidence-based decision factory that compares cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration workload by workload rather than forcing a cloud-first answer.

SAP-first manufacturing continuity: lead with credible SAP workload hosting, migration-risk control, RTO/RPO discipline, zero/low-downtime migration planning, and manufacturing/hospitality operational continuity, grounded in the comparable DEAL-003 manufacturing SAP win.

Open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency: present unit assumptions, transition costs, run costs, storage, backup, DR, network, security tooling, labor, savings trajectory, and scenario comparison clearly to avoid the DEAL-043 failure pattern where a stronger solution lost on price.

Compliance-by-design and no-disqualification governance: make certifications, DCaaS attestations, response-template completion, USD pricing, submission channel, and red-team compliance review a visible feature of the proposal, directly addressing losses in DEAL-056 and DEAL-100.

Accelerated, low-risk transition using reusable assets: emphasize workload placement scorecards, discovery accelerators, SAP/Oracle/database sizing models, VMware migration playbooks, landing-zone patterns, DR/security/observability blueprints, migration waves, rollback gates, and hypercare, reflecting win lessons from DEAL-007 and DEAL-060.
risk_factors: No public procurement data is available, so there is no ground-truth award-price benchmark or verified competitor win pattern for Kohler or comparable hosting/DCaaS awards. This lowers confidence and prevents an optimistic probability.

The strongest past proposal intelligence is from Kohler Project Polaris itself and appears to be WIP/generated proposal content, not evidence of a prior win. It is highly reusable but does not prove buyer preference or incumbent relationship.

The RFP has several eliminatory mechanics: due date/channel, mandatory Vendor Response template, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, and USD pricing. Historical losses DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 show this organization has lost comparable manufacturing IT bids by missing compliance appendix requirements.

Pricing risk is material. DEAL-043 shows a technically stronger enterprise IT proposal lost when priced 20% above the winner. For Polaris, a complex hybrid/cloud/DCaaS TCO could be vulnerable if assumptions are opaque or cost is not benchmarked.

Possible incumbent or ecosystem-provider advantage is unresolved. Past proposal language references vendor coexistence and incumbent accountabilities, but no intelligence confirms whether an incumbent, hyperscaler, DCaaS provider, or advisory firm has privileged access.

The scope spans Phase 0 advisory and potential downstream $15M-$75M hosting/managed services. Kohler may select a neutral advisor for Phase 0 and later compete implementation separately, limiting near-term revenue capture.

Detailed evaluation weights are not available. The extracted intelligence says scoring/weightage may be in a separate Evaluation Template/Excel, so win strategy could be misweighted unless that file is obtained and response is tailored to it.
must_fix_before_bidding: Create a mandatory compliance matrix mapping every eliminatory and must-have requirement to the exact response-template location, named owner, evidence artifact, and submission check. This directly addresses loss patterns from DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 where compliance appendix requirements were missed.

Attach current ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, cloud partner certifications, data center certifications, security attestations, and any DCaaS-provider compliance attestations required for the proposed scenarios.
Complete the Kohler Vendor Response template in detail with narrative answers, assumptions, evidence references, pricing in USD, and no unsupported Yes/No responses.

Build a USD-only commercial model separating Phase 0 advisory, landing-zone/setup, transition/migration, hosting/run, managed services, backup/DR, network connectivity, tooling, security, and optional cloud/DCaaS/hybrid scenarios over a 3-5 year horizon.

Include architecture diagrams and reference designs for public cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid options, including SAP, VMware, Windows/Linux, SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL/PostgreSQL, storage, backup/DR, IAM, monitoring/logging, and OT-adjacent read-only/controlled integrations.

Secure at least two direct US peer references, preferably manufacturing/SAP/data-center-exit or complex infrastructure references, because DEAL-020 shows US enterprise infrastructure buyers value direct peer validation.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: medium — Confidence: medium. HCLTech has strong U.S./global delivery capability and directly relevant VMware, DCaaS, SAP HyRISE, infrastructure, security and hybrid-cloud offerings [web], but no verified Wisconsin-specific job posting or recent Wisconsin contract win was found [job posting/web]. Public procurement data for this RFP/region was unavailable [public contract].
competitors.predicted_positioning: Likely angle: 'global hybrid-cloud transformation partner for SAP, VMware and DCaaS, with AI-enabled operations.' Evidence: HCLTech reports FY26 revenue of $14.7B, FY26 new-deal TCV of $9.3B, EBIT margin of 17.2%, AI Factory and AI infrastructure momentum, VMware DCaaS capability, Shared Data Center as a Service with IBM, SAP HyRISE, and enterprise infrastructure/cyber/network services [financial/web]. No recent public contract wins in Wisconsin found [web/public contract].
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Full program: likely $18M-$55M over 3-5 years; Phase 0: likely $400K-$1.1M. Confidence: medium-low because no public procurement award values were available. Calculation anchor: FY26 revenue/employee = $14.7B / 227,000 = about $64,800 per employee [financial/web]. EBIT margin = 17.2%, so pricing floor = cost / (1 - 0.172) = cost / 0.828 = 1.208x cost [financial]. Therefore a $18M bid implies an internal cost ceiling of about $14.9M, and a $55M bid implies cost ceiling of about $45.5M. Range is constrained by the user-provided enterprise hosting estimate of $15M-$75M and HCLTech's lower revenue/employee offshore/global delivery model; no public contract award anchor was available [RFP/public contract].
their_strengths: Proven scale: FY26 revenue $14.7B, 227,000+ people across 60 countries, 23,000+ U.S. professionals, and $9.3B FY26 new-deal TCV [financial/web].
Direct VMware and DCaaS relevance: dedicated VMware business unit, 9,000+ trained/certified VMware consultants, VMware Cloud on AWS, and Shared Data Center as a Service model [web].
SAP modernization relevance through HyRISE and enterprise applications transformation across finance, HR, supply chain and manufacturing [web].
Manufacturing relevance through digital manufacturing offerings integrating IT, OT and engineering systems, plus June 2026 automotive software partnership with e.solutions [web].
Recent AI/cloud momentum: AI Factory program over $100M, Red Hat AI infrastructure collaboration, ServiceNow/Google Cloud AI announcement, and Dell Americas partner recognition [web].
their_weaknesses: No verified Wisconsin-specific job posting or recent Wisconsin client/deal evidence found [job posting/web].
No public procurement award values were available for this opportunity, so their exact U.S./Wisconsin pricing behavior is not evidenced [public contract].
FY27 revenue guidance of only 1.0%-4.0% constant-currency growth may make them selective and margin-protective rather than purely price-aggressive [financial].

Their strongest public story is platform/alliance-led DCaaS, VMware, SAP and AI; Kohler can test whether they are truly neutral across DCaaS, hyperscaler, retain/modernize, and VMware-exit scenarios [web].
how_to_beat_them: Force the evaluation onto Wisconsin-local discovery quality, named Phase 0 team availability, and on-site workshops: source evidence shows HCLTech has 23,000+ U.S. professionals but no verified Wisconsin-specific job postings or Wisconsin client win found [job posting/web].

Exploit their likely platform-led VMware/SAP/DCaaS story by requiring a vendor-neutral decision model across retain, DCaaS, hyperscaler, SAP hosting, and VMware exit options; HCLTech evidence is strongest around its own VMware business unit, Shared DCaaS with IBM, HyRISE SAP modernization, and hybrid-cloud offerings [web].

Require detailed certification evidence and compliance attestations in the Kohler response template; HCLTech can likely provide global certifications, but the RFP disqualifier punishes generic yes/no responses and weak evidence [RFP].

Differentiate with local executive accountability and a Kohler-owned TCO/workload model; HCLTech has no public Wisconsin deal proof and will likely lean on global scale rather than local intimacy [web/job posting].

critical_success_factors: Detailed RFP compliance execution: the response must use Kohler’s Vendor Response template, avoid simple Yes/No answers, submit by 26 June, and route communications to the named Kohler addresses.

Mandatory compliance evidence: the RFP requires vendors to demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, and DCaaS providers must provide explicit compliance attestations.
Credible Cloud / DCaaS / Hybrid comparison: the solution must respond across relevant hosting scenarios and clearly state assumptions rather than forcing one target-state answer.

Target-state architecture depth: the response must cover multi-account / multi-subscription landing-zone principles, landing-zone deployment, application deployment model, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, hybrid connectivity, region strategy, data residency, DR, HA, and scalability.
Inventory-driven sizing and pricing: Kohler expects vendors to use the provided inventory dataset as the baseline for solution design and indicative pricing, with all gaps and assumptions disclosed.

Manufacturing-enterprise workload credibility: the in-scope estate includes SAP HANA, Oracle, SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL, VMware-style Windows/Linux estates, storage, backup/DR, IAM, network connectivity, security tooling, and selected OT-adjacent workloads.
Commercial transparency: pricing must be in USD, should address net-90 economics, and should separate optional services, deviations, dependencies, constraints, and exclusions.

Decision-lab positioning: the strongest provided proposal pattern is a 12-week vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab / Hosting Option Control Tower with open-book TCO, FinOps guardrails, workload placement scorecards, landing-zone blueprints, transition sequencing, rollback gates, and vendor-coexistence RACI.
common_failure_modes: Submitting generic Yes/No compliance answers instead of a detailed Vendor Response template response, which is explicitly insufficient under the RFP.
Missing the 26 June submission deadline or using the wrong communication channel, both of which are hard disqualifiers.
Failing to provide certification evidence or DCaaS compliance attestations where required.
Taking a biased cloud-first or DCaaS-first position instead of objectively comparing Cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, and Hybrid options workload by workload.
Building indicative pricing without using the provided inventory baseline dataset or without calling out data gaps and sizing assumptions.
Underestimating SAP HANA, Oracle, SQL Server, VMware, storage, backup/DR, network connectivity, monitoring, logging, and security tooling complexity.

Weak target-state architecture content: no landing-zone model, no environment segregation, no identity integration, no hybrid connectivity design, no region/data-residency approach, and no DR/HA/scalability patterns.
Ignoring manufacturing continuity and OT-adjacent constraints, especially plant connectivity, controlled integrations, latency, and operational disruption risk.
Pricing in a non-USD format or proposing payment terms materially less favorable than Kohler’s net-90 preference.
Failing to neutralize incumbent advantage by not showing a practical vendor-coexistence RACI and low-risk transition path.

No public procurement award values were available, so market validation relies on competitor price-to-win intelligence of $18M-$42M for a 3-5 year managed hybrid hosting/DCaaS/cloud transition. The $30.667M recommended price sits inside that band, aligns to the prior $30.667M Kohler variant, stays below likely Accenture/Deloitte/Kyndryl premium pricing, and avoids a low-margin/risky Rackspace-style floor. Math for recommended price: people/managed services $10.800M + infrastructure $7.780M + migration/transformation $2.200M + governance/tooling/compliance $1.524M + contingency $2.230M + margin $6.133M = $30.667M. Infrastructure uses live unit pricing scaled to enterprise size: AWS m5.xlarge $0.192/hr, m5.2xlarge $0.384/hr, r5.xlarge $0.252/hr, c5.xlarge $0.170/hr, t3.medium $0.0416/hr, Azure D16ds_v5 $0.904/hr, D4ds_v5 $0.410/hr, E2bs_v6 $0.266/hr, M128bds_v3 $11.710/hr, RDS db.m5.2xlarge PostgreSQL $0.924/hr, S3 $0.023/GB-month, RDS gp3 $0.115/GB-month, CloudWatch logs $0.500/GB and data transfer $0.090/GB. Confidence is moderate because the current-state Kohler inventory, exact RTO/RPOs and public award benchmarks are unavailable.

draft.executive_summary: Kohler's Project Polaris should not be treated as a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise. It is a resilience, cost, architecture, security, and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer whose Wisconsin data center, plants, SAP estate, hospitality operations, connected products, analytics platforms, and customer digital experiences must continue to perform while hosting options are compared. In the January 2026 Cognizant release, Eric Tabor's priorities centered on operational and business excellence through technology, productivity gains, faster response to business needs, and customer value through digital engineering and domain expertise. Our response makes Polaris a risk-managed Hosting Decision Lab: a fixed-fee, vendor-neutral Phase 0 that lets Kohler own the evidence, compare cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration workload by workload, and leave with executable sourcing artifacts instead of downstream lock-in.
win_themes: Vendor-neutral Polaris Hosting Decision Lab: Kohler-owned, evidence-based decisioning across cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration options.
SAP-first manufacturing continuity: disciplined hosting and migration planning for SAP, RTO/RPO, zero or low-downtime options, and manufacturing and hospitality continuity.
Open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency: visible assumptions, unit costs, transition costs, run costs, savings trajectory, and three-to-five-year scenario comparison.
Compliance-by-design and no-disqualification governance: response completion, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, submission checks, and red-team compliance review.

Accelerated, low-risk transition using reusable assets: scorecards, discovery accelerators, sizing models, VMware playbooks, landing-zone patterns, DR/security/observability blueprints, migration waves, rollback gates, and hypercare.

sections.section_title: Target state architecture, security and governance capabilities
sections.content: The target state is a governed hybrid hosting architecture, not a preselected destination. We will baseline Kohler's SAP, VMware, database, manufacturing, hospitality, analytics, connected-product, and integration patterns, then score each workload against business criticality, latency, regulatory exposure, security controls, data gravity, support model, RTO/RPO, and three-to-five-year USD TCO. The Phase 0 deliverables include landing-zone patterns, retained data-center options, DCaaS reference architecture, hybrid plant/edge patterns, IAM and privileged-access model, cyber control mapping, observability blueprint, certification evidence matrix, and shared-responsibility RACI. This directly advances the vendor-neutral Polaris Hosting Decision Lab and compliance-by-design win themes by making security, governance, and no-disqualification evidence part of the architecture package. Kohler finishes with target-state choices it can defend, source, and govern without surrendering provider optionality.

sections.section_title: Migration approach, risk considerations and transition strategy
sections.content: Our migration strategy protects manufacturing and hospitality continuity before it optimizes platform preference. We will create SAP/VMware dependency maps, Oracle and database sizing models, application grouping logic, network and identity dependencies, integration inventories, and migration-wave candidates. Each wave will include entry criteria, rehearsal needs, cutover approach, rollback gate, data-protection method, testing scope, hypercare model, and decision owner. For SAP and other tier-one workloads, the plan will apply the SAP-first manufacturing continuity theme: RTO/RPO discipline, migration-risk scoring, zero or low-downtime options where justified, and explicit blackout handling for plants, distribution, hospitality properties, digital commerce, and customer service. Reusable migration playbooks, discovery accelerators, landing-zone patterns, and rollback gates reduce transition risk while leaving Kohler with an implementation-ready plan that any selected downstream provider can execute.

Polaris depends on the network as much as the hosting platform. We will document WAN, internet, VPN, private connectivity, plant/edge, data-center, DCaaS, cloud, SaaS, DNS, routing, firewall, segmentation, remote-access, and third-party integration requirements, then design a hybrid-connectivity target state that supports phased coexistence. The analysis will compare latency-sensitive manufacturing workloads, SAP integration traffic, hospitality and guest-facing dependencies, backup and replication paths, administrative access, and security inspection points across hosting scenarios. Network cost assumptions will be shown in the open-book USD TCO, including circuits, egress, cross-connects, appliance or virtual firewall costs, monitoring, and labor. This reinforces the vendor-neutral decision lab and FinOps transparency themes: Kohler sees the operational and commercial impact of connectivity choices before committing to cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, or hybrid placement.

sections.section_title: Availability, resilience and disaster recovery approach
sections.content: We will make resilience a placement criterion, not a remediation task after sourcing. Each workload will be assigned an availability tier with current and target RTO/RPO, dependency tolerance, backup frequency, recovery sequence, data replication pattern, test cadence, monitoring need, and business owner validation. The target-state DR package will compare active-active, active-passive, backup-restore, DCaaS recovery, hyperscaler recovery, retained data-center recovery, and plant/edge survivability options where relevant. AIOps observability, aligned to the Neuro IT Ops reliability and observability scope identified in client intelligence, will be incorporated into event correlation, service health, alert routing, and SLA reporting. This section carries the SAP-first continuity and accelerated low-risk transition themes: Kohler receives DR runbooks, resilience tiers, migration rollback gates, and hypercare requirements before implementation begins, reducing cutover uncertainty and operational exposure.

We will provide an open-book USD TCO model that exposes the assumptions behind every scenario. The model will separate transition costs, discovery and design fees, migration execution, run labor, hosting, storage, backup, DR, network, security tooling, observability, licensing impacts, managed services, retained operations, decommissioning, savings timing, inflation assumptions, and contingency. It will compare cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration options over three-to-five years with unit rates and workload-level attribution. This directly addresses the open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency theme and avoids the price ambiguity pattern identified in prior losses. The Phase 0 is proposed as fixed-fee and vendor-neutral, producing Kohler-owned commercial artifacts and an RFP-ready sourcing pack that can be handed to any hyperscaler, DCaaS provider, SI, or managed-services provider.

The future hosting choice must fit Kohler's operating model rather than force one. We will map current service ownership, incident paths, change controls, escalation routes, SAP basis touchpoints, cybersecurity roles, plant support, application ownership, vendor responsibilities, and managed-service interfaces. The target model will include ITIL and SRE-aligned practices, service catalog, RACI, escalation matrix, change calendar, release governance, vulnerability and patch workflow, observability operations, service-level reporting, and hypercare-to-run handoff. We will explicitly address coexistence with incumbent and future providers so Polaris does not create accountability gaps during transition. This reinforces compliance-by-design governance and accelerated low-risk transition: Kohler gets a run-ready operating model, support boundaries, and governance cadence that preserve productivity, speed business response, and keep ownership of the decision evidence.

We will evaluate platforms through maturity evidence, not sales preference. The scorecard will assess discovery tooling, CMDB and dependency mapping, SAP and database sizing evidence, VMware migration methods, landing-zone automation, policy-as-code, identity integration, backup and DR tooling, security telemetry, AIOps observability, FinOps reporting, ticketing integration, and ecosystem certification evidence. Reusable assets include workload placement scorecards, discovery accelerators, SAP/Oracle/database sizing models, VMware migration playbooks, landing-zone patterns, DR, security, and observability blueprints, migration-wave templates, rollback gates, and hypercare checklists. This advances the reusable-asset and vendor-neutral themes: Kohler can compare provider maturity on the same evidence base, identify gaps before award, and avoid being guided by a competitor's pull-through agenda rather than Polaris outcomes.

Our deliverables are designed as executable assets, not advisory slides. Phase 0 will produce workload-by-workload placement recommendations, SAP/VMware dependency maps, application and infrastructure baselines, target landing-zone and DCaaS designs, hybrid-connectivity design, IAM and security-control model, compliance and certification evidence matrix, backup and DR target state, RTO/RPO tiers, migration waves, rollback gates, runbook outline, shared-responsibility RACI, open-book USD TCO, sourcing assumptions, and RFP-ready provider package. Each artifact will have an owner, acceptance criterion, source evidence, and downstream use. This is the killer differentiator: Kohler owns the evidence and can hand the package to any provider without lock-in. The outcome is a decision factory that supports operational excellence and faster response to business needs.

The supplied RFP decomposition states that detailed weightages and scoring structure reside in an External Evaluation Template that was not included. We will therefore manage Polaris against every listed criterion with equal compliance discipline until Kohler releases the Excel scoring model, then recalibrate response depth, evidence, and pricing detail to match the final weights. Our compliance-by-design approach includes a response-template completion check, USD pricing validation, certification and DCaaS attestation matrix, submission-channel review, and red-team no-disqualification gate. This protects Kohler and the bid from preventable scoring leakage while preserving the vendor-neutral, open-book decision narrative.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Pursue this as a strategic Phase 0 advisory wedge into a larger hosting transformation and managed-services opportunity. Lead with an unbiased decision framework across Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid, but differentiate through granular TCO transparency, SAP/non-SAP workload treatment, compliance evidence, and a practical risk-managed migration roadmap for Kohler’s Wisconsin data-center estate and global manufacturing operations.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: Win by making Kohler confident that the Phase 0 output will be decision-grade: a certification-backed, vendor-neutral Cloud/DCaaS/Hybrid comparison with transparent SAP and non-SAP TCO, realistic migration risk analysis, and a credible operating model for global manufacturing resilience.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid pricing strategy: fixed-fee, milestone-based pricing for Phase 0 advisory, paired with transparent 3-5 year scenario-based TCO models for Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options. Price competitively but not purely lowest-cost: Kohler is explicitly evaluating architecture, migration risk, resilience, governance, operating model, and commercial transparency, so the model should show itemized SAP versus non-SAP costs, setup/migration/managed-services run costs, network/security/backup/DR costs, assumptions, cost protections, and optimization savings over time. Include net-90 payment terms, all values in USD, clear optional add-ons, and a mechanism to credit part of Phase 0 fees toward subsequent migration or managed-services phases if awarded.
key_risks: The Evaluation Template, Vendor Response template, and inventory baseline are missing; they may contain hidden scoring weights, mandatory fields, workload counts, certification requirements, and disqualifiers that materially change the response strategy.

This is a Phase 0 evaluation rather than a full implementation award, so the proposal must be advisory-quality and commercially credible without overcommitting to final architecture or pricing before inventory validation.

Kohler explicitly requires transparent pricing, SAP/non-SAP cost separation, cost increase protections, efficiency gains, and net-90 terms; weak commercial structure could lose even with a strong technical solution.
Compliance evidence and DCaaS attestations are hard disqualifiers; any missing certification proof, vague compliance claim, or unsupported data-residency assumption could make the bid non-compliant.

The scope spans enterprise IT and manufacturing/OT-adjacent connectivity; underestimating plant network constraints, latency, security segmentation, or controlled OT integration risks could weaken the migration strategy.
SAP HANA, Oracle, SQL Server, file storage, backup/DR, and VMware dependencies may create complex migration sequencing and licensing risks, especially under changing VMware/Broadcom economics.

Competitors may attempt to lock Kohler into a preferred hyperscaler or proprietary DCaaS model; the response must show credible neutrality across Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options while still providing a clear recommendation framework.
Submission logistics are strict: due by 26 June, through specified contacts, using the required template, with detailed answers rather than Yes/No responses, and all pricing in USD.

gaps: Competitor intelligence is materially incomplete: only HCLTech is visible, while obvious likely bidders/incumbents such as Cognizant, Kyndryl, DXC, TCS, Infosys, Wipro, IBM, Accenture, and hyperscaler/DCaaS partners are not assessed.

Competitor pricing rationale is weak for HCLTech: revenue-per-employee and EBIT margin do not credibly anchor a $18M-$55M managed hosting/DCaaS bid. Need comparable managed infrastructure, data center outsourcing, SAP hosting, or cloud transformation deal benchmarks.

Cognizant appears to be a strategic incumbent/partner for Kohler, but the competitor analysis does not evaluate Cognizant’s incumbent advantage, potential conflict, or how to position around coexistence/displacement.

WIN_INTEL output is truncated and does not show a final win probability, capability-gap assessment, or calibrated rationale. Similar-deal lessons are useful, but the decisioning layer is missing from the visible output.

CLIENT_INTEL is directionally strong and specific, but it leans heavily on strategic partnership/news signals. It should be treated as good enough with a note that the proposal must not overstate unsupported budget authority or assume Cognizant displacement.
strengths: Client intel uses specific web-derived signals rather than generic industry trends.
Unstated needs are actionable: hybrid-cloud decision framework, AIOps/observability, security-by-design, FinOps/TBM, privacy governance, and Cognizant coexistence.
Win-intel similar deals are relevant to manufacturing, SAP, compliance gates, and 3-5 year transformation programs.
RFP disqualifiers are clearly captured and should materially improve compliance discipline.

agentic_decisions.reasoning: Pursue this as a strategic Phase 0 advisory wedge into a larger hosting transformation and managed-services opportunity. Lead with an unbiased decision framework across Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid, but differentiate through granular TCO transparency, SAP/non-SAP workload treatment, compliance evidence, and a practical risk-managed migration roadmap for Kohler’s Wisconsin data-center estate and global manufacturing operations.

The client intelligence contains real, specific signals and actionable unstated needs, especially around hybrid cloud, AIOps, security, FinOps, IoT/privacy, and Cognizant coexistence. The win-intel similar deals are relevant and provide useful lessons around manufacturing SAP scale and compliance gates. However, the competitor intelligence is not good enough for a proposal strategy on a $15M-$75M hosting options RFP: it visibly covers only HCLTech, misses the likely incumbent Cognizant and other major infrastructure/cloud outsourcing competitors, and uses a pricing methodology that is too indirect to support competitive bid positioning. The visible WIN_INTEL output is also incomplete/truncated before providing a calibrated win probability. These gaps would materially affect win strategy, differentiation, and pricing narrative, so proceeding now risks building a proposal around an under-informed competitive thesis.
